learn.title: Wind Turbine Lifespan & Recycling
learn.intro: Wind turbines typically last 20-25 years, but blade recycling is a growing challenge. Proper end-of-life management reduces waste and supports circular economy goals, crucial for sustainable energy transitions.
learn.c1_text: Wind turbines operate for 20-25 years before needing replacement. Maintenance costs ~1-2% of initial investment annually. Blades, made of composite materials, are hard to recycle due to mixed materials. For example, a 100 MW turbine produces 300 tons of blade waste at end-of-life.
learn.c2_text: Regular maintenance ensures efficiency but adds costs. Sensors monitor blade health, while technicians replace worn parts. In India, 60% of turbines face delays in maintenance due to remote locations, increasing downtime.
learn.c3_text: Blade recycling is emerging in India. The National Mission on Transformative Recycling aims to recycle 50% of blades by 2030. Companies like NTPC are piloting processes to repurpose materials into construction aggregates.
learn.stat1: India: 5% blade recycling rate (2023), vs. 90% for other turbine components
learn.stat2: Global blade waste: 1.5 million tons/year by 2030 (IRENA)
learn.stat3: India's target: Recycle 200,000 tons of blades by 2030
learn.stat4: Cost to recycle one blade: $10,000-15,000 (vs. $5,000 disposal)

learn.title: EV Charging Levels Explained
learn.intro: EV charging infrastructure varies by speed and cost. Level 1 uses home outlets, Level 2 adds faster charging, and DC fast chargers enable quick top-ups, critical for mass EV adoption and grid integration.
learn.c1_text: Level 1 charging uses standard 120V outlets, adding ~3-5 miles of range per hour. Ideal for overnight charging at home. A 60 kWh EV would take 12 hours to fully charge this way.
learn.c2_text: Level 2 uses 240V, adding 10-30 miles/hour. Common in public stations and workplaces. India’s FAME scheme mandates Level 2 chargers in new EV purchases, boosting urban charging access.
learn.c3_text: DC fast chargers (150kW+) add 80% range in 20-30 minutes. Key for long trips. India has 1,200+ DC chargers (2024), but rural areas lack coverage. Cost: $0.20-0.50/kWh vs. $0.10/kWh for Level 2.
learn.stat2: DC fast chargers: 150+ kW average power (2023)
learn.stat3: Home Level 1: 70% of Indian EV owners use it
learn.stat4: DC fast charging: 3x cost of Level 2 per kWh

learn.title: Microgrids for Energy Independence
learn.intro: Microgrids allow villages to generate and manage their own power, reducing reliance on central grids. They enhance disaster resilience by operating independently during outages, crucial for remote or vulnerable communities.
learn.c1_text: Microgrids combine solar, wind, or diesel to power a community. A 100-household village in Rajasthan uses a 50 kW solar microgrid, cutting diesel costs by 60% and providing 24/7 power.
learn.c2_text: During monsoons or grid failures, microgrids isolate and maintain power. Post-2023 floods in Odisha, microgrids in coastal areas kept hospitals and communication systems running.
learn.c3_text: India has 1,500+ microgrids (2024), with targets to reach 10,000 by 2030. The government’s PM-KUSUM scheme supports solar microgrids in agriculture and rural areas.
learn.stat3: Disaster savings: $2M/year in avoided losses (Odisha case)

learn.title: Geothermal Energy Basics
learn.intro: Geothermal energy taps Earth’s internal heat for power and heating. It’s a reliable, low-emission source, ideal for baseload power and reducing fossil fuel dependence in suitable regions.
learn.c1_text: Geothermal plants use steam or hot water from underground reservoirs. A 100 MW plant can produce 900 GWh/year. Iceland generates 30% of its electricity this way.
learn.c2_text: Geothermal heat pumps provide efficient heating/cooling. In India, 50,000+ homes use geothermal systems for space heating, reducing energy use by 40% compared to traditional methods.
learn.c3_text: India has 10,000 MW geothermal potential, mostly in Rajasthan and Tamil Nadu. The National Thermal Power Corporation (NTPC) runs a 10 MW plant in Churu, Rajasthan, supplying power to 100,000 homes.

learn.title: Capacity Factor Demystified
learn.intro: Capacity factor measures how much energy a plant actually produces vs. its maximum potential. It highlights why renewable projects need careful planning, as wind and solar output varies with weather, affecting grid reliability and economics.
learn.c1_text: Capacity factor = Actual output / Max possible output. A 20% wind turbine capacity factor means it produces 20% of its rated power over time. Solar in India averages 15-20% due to weather variability.
learn.c2_text: Low capacity factors increase costs. A 10 MW solar plant with 15% capacity factor needs 667 MW of capacity to match a 100 MW plant at 15%. This affects grid balancing and storage needs.
learn.c3_text: India’s solar capacity factor averages 15-18%. In Rajasthan, it’s 22% due to high solar irradiance. Wind farms in Tamil Nadu have 25-30% capacity factors, making them more efficient.

learn.title: Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) Technology
learn.intro: V2G allows EVs to supply power back to the grid during peak demand. This technology can stabilize grids, reduce curtailment, and turn EVs into mobile energy storage, enhancing renewable integration and grid resilience.
learn.c1_text: V2G uses smart inverters to convert DC battery power to AC grid power. A 60 kWh EV can supply 5-10 kW to the grid for 1-2 hours. This requires vehicle and grid compatibility standards.
learn.c2_text: During peak demand, V2G EVs can reduce grid strain. In Germany, V2G pilots have cut peak loads by 15% during heatwaves. India’s EV policies could leverage this for urban grid management.
learn.c3_text: India has 20M+ EVs (2024). V2G could add 10 GW of flexible capacity by 2030. The government’s Faster Adoption and Manufacturing (FAME) scheme includes V2G research, with pilot projects in Delhi and Bengaluru.
